architecture.summary: CDSL combines constrained decoding with speculative decoding. A small draft LLM autoregressively generates speculative lookahead tokens. These tokens are validated by a larger target LLM via forward pass (hard rejection or speculative sampling) and scored by a task-specific reward function. Based on acceptance and reward scores, the system decides via state-based rules whether to accept drafted tokens, sample from the target LLM with constraint guidance, or fall back to CDLH-approximate beam search. The process repeats until target sequence length is reached.

claims.statement: CDSL achieves 2.2x to 12.15x speedup over CDLH without significant performance reduction
On CommonGen, CDSL achieves hard constraint satisfaction gain compared to greedy decoding
On Harmless Text Generation, CDSL achieves gain compared to greedy decoding
CDLH-Appx. baseline outperforms greedy by in CommonGen and in HTG tasks
Hard rejection achieves slightly better speedup compared to speculative sampling, but hard rejection consistently achieves higher performance
Nucleus sampling does not improve constraint satisfaction performance; increased diversity does not contribute to better adherence to constraints
CDSL shows a runtime-performance tradeoff compared to the performance skyline CDLH; however, the performance tradeoff is not drastic compared to the runtime skyline greedy decoding
Approach requires target and draft models from the same LLM family due to tokenizer mismatch; this is a general limitation of speculative decoding-based approaches
